questions.q: A person is in need of money for his business and he knows that he will get the payment from a customer within a short period. He asks his banker to draw a bill on his customer. This is an example of:
questions.answer: This is an example of a 'bill of exchange' drawn at sight. A bill of exchange is a written order by one person (drawer) to another person (drawee) directing him to pay a certain sum of money to a third person (payee) either on demand or at a specified future date. In this case, the bill is drawn at sight, meaning that the drawee is required to pay the amount immediately upon presentation of the bill.
marking_scheme: Identify the type of bill (at sight) - 1 mark
Explain the purpose of a bill of exchange - 2 marks
Describe the parties involved in a bill of exchange - 2 marks

questions.q: What are the essential elements of a bill of exchange? (a) Date, (b) Drawee's name, (c) Amount of money, (d) Payee's name, (e) Signature of the drawer. State the marks allocated to each part.
questions.answer: The essential elements of a bill of exchange are: (a) Date - 1 mark (the date on which the bill is drawn), (b) Drawee's name - 1 mark (the person on whom the bill is drawn), (c) Amount of money - 1 mark (the amount of money that is to be paid), (d) Payee's name - 1 mark (the person to whom the money is to be paid), and (e) Signature of the drawer - 1 mark (the signature of the person drawing the bill).
marking_scheme: Identify the drawee's name element - 1 mark
Identify the amount of money element - 1 mark
Identify the payee's name element - 1 mark
Identify the signature of the drawer element - 1 mark
questions.topic: Essential Elements of a Bill of Exchange

questions.q: State the differences between a promissory note and a bill of exchange. (a) A bill of exchange is drawn by the buyer, while a promissory note is drawn by the seller. (b) A bill of exchange is drawn at sight, while a promissory note is drawn at a specified future date. (c) A bill of exchange is drawn for the payment of a sum of money, while a promissory note is drawn for the payment of goods. (d) A bill of exchange is payable to the order of a person, while a promissory note is payable to bearer. (e) All of the above.
questions.answer: The correct statement is (e) All of the above. A bill of exchange and a promissory note are both documents used for the payment of money, but they are drawn by different parties and have different characteristics. A bill of exchange is drawn by a buyer or seller for the payment of a sum of money, is drawn at sight, and is payable to the order of a person. A promissory note is also drawn for the payment of a sum of money, but is drawn by a debtor for the payment of a debt, is drawn at a specified future date, and is payable to bearer.
marking_scheme: Explain the difference in parties involved - 1 mark
Explain the difference in terms of time of payment - 1 mark
Explain the difference in terms of purpose - 1 mark
Explain the difference in terms of payment - 1 mark
questions.topic: Bill of Exchange vs. Promissory Note

questions.q: What is the effect of dishonor of a bill of exchange on the drawer and the drawee? (a) The drawer is discharged from liability, while the drawee is liable for payment. (b) The drawer is liable for payment, while the drawee is discharged from liability. (c) Both the drawer and the drawee are discharged from liability. (d) Both the drawer and the drawee are liable for payment.
questions.answer: The correct answer is (a) The drawer is discharged from liability, while the drawee is liable for payment. When a bill of exchange is dishonored, the drawer is discharged from liability, meaning that they are no longer responsible for payment. However, the drawee is still liable for payment, as they are the one who was supposed to pay the amount.
marking_scheme: Explain the effect on the drawer - 2 marks
Explain the effect on the drawee - 2 marks
questions.topic: Dishonor of a Bill of Exchange

questions.q: Explain the different types of bills of exchange. (a) Bill at sight, (b) Bill for a specified future date, (c) Bill for a fixed period, (d) All of the above.
questions.answer: The correct answer is (d) All of the above. There are three main types of bills of exchange, each with its own characteristics. A bill at sight is a bill that is drawn for immediate payment, a bill for a specified future date is a bill that is drawn for payment on a specific date in the future, and a bill for a fixed period is a bill that is drawn for payment within a specific time frame.
marking_scheme: Explain the bill at sight - 2 marks
Explain the bill for a specified future date - 2 marks
Explain the bill for a fixed period - 1 mark
